coredata.dc:title: Encapsulating carbon quantum dot and organic dye in multi-shell nanostructured MOFs for use in white light-emitting diode
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yantong
Wang, Shuo
Yu, Kuangli
Yu, Jiulong
Zhao, Dian
Li, Chunxia
coredata.dc:description: Encapsulating luminescence units into porous metal-organic frameworks to obtain white-light emission has attracted much attention because of their high designability and excellent color-tuning flexibility. In this study, two types of luminescence units with different emission color coverage, namely green-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and red-emitting rhodamine B (RhB) molecules, were encapsulated into porous ZIF-8 to obtain the yellow-emitting composite phosphor for use in white light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82, as well as single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82, were fabricated and fine-tuned (by varying the amount of accommodated CQDs and RhB) so that the emitted color is near that of the commercial yellow-emitting phosphor. The multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 has much better luminescence efficiency than that of the single-shell one. This is because the multi-shell structure provides a much larger separation distance of the encapsulated guest units, diminishing the energy-transfer-caused efficiency decrease. It is also shown that a bright source of white light can be realized by coating the CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposite on a commercial blue (460 nm) LED chip.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White-light emission
Metal-organic frameworks
Core-shell nanostructures
Host-guest hybrid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51802284 , 51872263 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LY20E020007 , LZ19E020001 ).
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51802284, 51872263, 52050077), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY20E020007, LZ19E020001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Structural dimension of the host framework of ZIF-8. (b) Chemical structures and molecular dimensions of rhodamine B (RhB).

(a) Powder XRD spectra of simulated ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8, RhB@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-82, RhB@ZIF-82, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82, and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82. (b) FTIR spectra of pure ZIF-8, CQDs, RhB, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82, and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 powder samples.
The SEM images of (a) CQDs@ZIF-8, (b) CQDs@ZIF-82, (c) CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-8, (d) CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82.

Emission spectra of CQDs@ZIF-82 powder samples (a) and RhB@ZIF-82 powder samples (b) under different excitation wavelength from 360 to 460 nm. (c) The overlap between emission spectrum of CQDs methanol solution (green line) and absorption spectrum of RhB methanol solution (red line). (d) Illustration of energy transfer pathways in CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Emission spectra (a) and the CIE coordinates (c) of CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 powder samples under excitation at 460 nm. Emission spectra (b) and the CIE coordinates (d) of CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 powder samples (excited at 460 nm).

Photographs of white light-emitting LEDs fabricated by coating the B3 on a blue (460 nm) LED chip when the blue LED is turned off (left) and turned on (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram showing the synthetic and assembly process of CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 (mode 1) and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 (mode 2)

head.ce:title: Encapsulating carbon quantum dot and organic dye in multi-shell nanostructured MOFs for use in white light-emitting diode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials, Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua, 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials, Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua, 321004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Encapsulating luminescence units into porous metal-organic frameworks to obtain white-light emission has attracted much attention because of their high designability and excellent color-tuning flexibility. In this study, two types of luminescence units with different emission color coverage, namely green-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and red-emitting rhodamine B (RhB) molecules, were encapsulated into porous ZIF-8 to obtain the yellow-emitting composite phosphor for use in white light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82, as well as single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82, were fabricated and fine-tuned (by varying the amount of accommodated CQDs and RhB) so that the emitted color is near that of the commercial yellow-emitting phosphor. The multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 has much better luminescence efficiency than that of the single-shell one. This is because the multi-shell structure provides a much larger separation distance of the encapsulated guest units, diminishing the energy-transfer-caused efficiency decrease. It is also shown that a bright source of white light can be realized by coating the CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposite on a commercial blue (460 nm) LED chip.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, solid-state white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs) have been investigated and widely applied in lighting, display and backlight because of their high luminous efficiency, long lifetime, low energy consumption, environmental friendliness and reliability [1–4]. They were considered as one of the most promising lighting sources of this century, which was able to replace the traditional halogen, fluorescent and incandescent lamps. The most straightforward way to achieve WLEDs is the RGB technique involving the combination of red, green and blue LED chips; however, the complicated assembly process and the high cost significantly restricted its large-scale application. An alternative strategy is to coat a single chip with phosphors. For instance, the employment of a blue- or UV-emitting chip assembled with a yellow- or multi-phase white-emitting phosphors [5,6]. The latter not only suffers from poor color stability and reproducibility caused by the different degradation rates of the individual phosphor, but also has the risk of UV radiation leakage which may do harm to human health [7]. In contrast, the blue-emitting chips coupled with single-phase phosphors with yellow-light emission are capable of providing better color stability and reproducibility, as well as much simpler manufacturing procedures, which are regarded as the most promising method toward the fabrication of WLEDs [8–10]. Thus, the design and synthesis of novel blue-light-excitable single-phase phosphors has emerged a very hot research topic in the past years. Moreover, the increasing need for mini optoelectronic devices is driving the development of novel nanophosphors. Despite already much investigated, exploring high-quality single-phase nanophosphors is still a big challenge.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a class of highly ordered crystalline materials constructed by metal ions (or clusters) and organic ligands, have attracted wide attention in gas storage and separation [11–13], catalysis [14–16], drug delivery [17,18], pollutant removal [19,20] and chemical sensing [21–23] due to their highly structural designability and intriguing functionalities. Specifically, the unique advantages including ultrahigh porosities and surface area and tunable pore structures make MOFs the promising host matrices to developing multifunctional luminescent materials and electroluminescent devices [24–30]. For example, several electroluminescent MOFs have been shown to be excellent candidates as emissive layers for fabrication of white light-emitting LEDs [31,32]. In addition, a number of luminescence guest units, such as, organic dyes, polyoxometalates, up-conversion nanoparticles, carbon and semiconductor quantum dots, have been encapsulated into MOFs to construct many advanced MOF/guest composite materials for various optical applications, especially in solid-state lighting and display [33–35]. Since the first demonstration on dyes@MOF-based phosphor for WLED in 2014 [36], great efforts have been devoted to enhance the white-light emission performance by changing the MOF structure and dye molecules [37–40]. The reported dyes@MOF hybrids often show good white-light emission property; however, some limitations such as the mismatched size between the host pores and the guest dyes, and the leakage of the dyes hindered the widespread application of this strategy. Furthermore, the interactions among the host and different guest dyes, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), might impede the white-light-emission and decrease the efficiency. Very recently, Gong et al. reported a novel approach to achieve white-light-emission by encapsulating red-, green-, and blue-emitting dyes into different layers of the host MOFs [41]. Li et al. developed a facile solution-processable to encapsulate multi-color dyes into the different layers of nanocrystalline MOF to form white-light emitting system [42]. In these researches, however, the excitation light sources are all high-energy UV light, which could limit the large-scale commercial applications. In this regard, further improvements in fabricating blue-light-excitable MOF-based host-guest composites with a simple synthetic procedure, high reproducibility and processability, and excellent white-light emission are of great significance.

Zeolitic imidazolate framework 8 (ZIF-8) is a well-known nanoscale MOF and shows excellent chemical and mechanical stability, cheap raw material, and easy of synthesis, as well as suitable pore cavities for the accommodation of the luminescence units, which can be an ideal host matrix to developing nanoscale host-guest hybrid phosphor. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and red-emitting rhodamine B (RhB) were chosen as the guest luminescence units due to their merits of high fluorescence efficiency, wide emission spectra and favorable stability [43]. Herein, two typical luminescence units with different color emission coverage, green-emitting CQDs and red-emitting RhB, are encapsulated into ZIF-8 cavities through a facile solution-processable method to from a blue-light-excitable host-guest hybrid system. To investigate and control the effect of the spatial distance between guest units on luminescence, two modes are presented, namely, single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 (model 1), single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 (model 2) (Scheme 1 ). We speculated that, with the blue-light excitation, white-light emission can be achieved in both of the two models by varying the amounts of CQDs and RhB molecules, and the multi-shell nanostructure of mode 2 can effectively alleviate the FRET-caused efficiency decrease between CQDs and RhB dyes by isolating them in the different shells of the nanostructure. As expected, the single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 shows a significant improvement in luminescence efficiency than that of the single-shell one, and which CIE chromaticity coordinate is very close to the representative YAG: Ce3+ phosphor. As such, a bright source of white light could be readily realized by coating the multi-shell nanocomposite on a commercial blue LED (460 nm) chip.

All chemicals, solvents, and reagents from commercial sources were of analytical grade and used as received. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-synthesized samples were recorded in the 2θ range of 3-50° using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired on a ZEISS GeminiSEM 300 with an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were conducted on a PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 using KBr pellets. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was conducted on a Malvem Zetasizer nano ZS90. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was performed on Hitachi UH5300 spectrophotometer. All excitation and emission spectra of the powder solid samples were collected using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room conditions, with a scan speed at 240 nm per min. Both the excitation and emission slits are 2.5 nm. The quantum yields of the samples were evaluated using an Edinburgh Instruments FS-5 spectrometer configured with an BaSO4-coating integrated sphere. The luminescence decay curves of the nanocomposite samples were recorded using a 450 nm LED laser. The electroluminescence spectrum of the fabricated LED was measured using a PMS-80 UV-VIS-near IR spectrophotocolorimeter.

Synthesis of CQDs: A 12 mL ultrapure aqueous solution containing 3 g citric acid and 4 g urea was stirred for 10 min, and then was placed in a microwave reactor. After microwave heating at the power of 700 W for 5 min, a brownish suspension was obtained. After the addition of 100 mL methanol, the resultant mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to remove any large size of particles, and the supernatant containing CQDs was preserved.

Synthesis of CQDs@ZIF-8: The organic ligand 2-methylimidazole (0.45 g) and 0.1 mg cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved in 25 mL methanol under ultrasonic waves, followed by the addition of 2 mL CQDs solution and 25 mL of 0.45 g Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O methanol solution. The mixture was transferred to a sealed round bottom flask and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The white product CQDs@ZIF-8 was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min and washed with methanol several times until no absorption peak of CQDs was detected in the replaced methanol solution. The obtained powder sample was dried in an over at 50 °C for 6 h.

Synthesis of RhB@ZIF-8: The organic ligand 2-methylimidazole (0.45g) and 1 mg CTAB was dissolved in 25 mL methanol under ultrasonic waves, followed by the addition of 25 mL of 0.45 g Zn(CH3COO)2·6H2O methanol solution. After stirring about 30 s, the different amounts (20, 40, 120, 200, and 240 μL) of 5 mM RhB aqueous solution were added. The mixture was stirred for another 60 s and then let stand for 2 h at room temperature. The product was collected through centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min and washed with methanol several times until no absorption peak of RhB molecule was detected in the replaced methanol solution. The obtained powder sample was dried in an over at 50 °C for 6 h.

Synthesis of CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8: 1 mg CTAB was stirred with 5 mL 2.72 M 2-methylimidazole a for 1 min, then 5 mL of 600 mg Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O aqueous solution was added. After stirring for a few seconds, different amount (120, 200, and 240 μL) of 5 mM RhB methanol solution and 3 mL CQDs solution was dropped, and the mixture continued to stir for 2 min, then let set the mixture for 2 h at room temperature. The resulting CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8 composites were collected by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min and wash with methanol several times washed with methanol several times until no absorption peak of CQDs and RhB molecules were detected in the replaced methanol solution. The obtained powder sample was dried in an over at 50 °C for 6 h.

Synthesis of CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8
2
: The as-synthesized CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8 was redispersed into 20 mL methanol solution containing 30 mM 2-methylimidazole, and then 20 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O methanol solution (30 mM) was added. Then let set the mixture for 1 h, and the formed CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The white solid powder was washed with methanol several times and dried in an over at 50 °C for 6 h.

Synthesis of CQDs@ZIF-8
2
@RhB@ZIF-8
2
: As-obtained CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-8 was redispersed in 30 mL of 30 mM 2-methylimidazole methanol solution. After being shaken for 60 s, 30 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O methanol solution (30 mM) was added. Then let set the mixture for 1 h at room temperature. The formed CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 was harvested by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min and washed with methanol several times, and dried in oven at 50 °C.

Fabrication of WLED: Certain amounts of transparent epoxy resin and curing agent were mixed together with the CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 composite materials (weight ratio = 1:4:1), and the mixture was stirred for 10 min to reach a homogenous state. Then the mixture was coated on a blue LED chip, and solidified in an oven at 90 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of as-synthesized compounds
ce:section.ce:para: ZIF-8, assembled from Zn2+ ions and 2-methylimidazole linkers, is one of the most classic nanoscale MOFs and features excellent chemical and mechanical stability, good biocompatibility, cheap raw material, and easy of synthesis, which is highly conducive to large-scale production and mini-device construction [8]. As exhibited in Fig. 1 a, the large cage size (11.6 Å) of ZIF-8 enables it an ideal host material that can capture guest luminescent molecules within the orderly arranged pores and avoid the aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ), while the limited aperture (3.4 Å) of the pore window can effectively prevent the leakage of guest molecules from the cages, thus affording a stable and isolated condition of accommodation, away from external distraction. Besides, the simple and mild synthesis of ZIF-8 make it suitable for use as coating layer to encapsulate the luminescent nanoparticles and fabricate the hierarchical nanoarchitectures toward various optical applications [34]. To obtain a yellow nanophosphor with broadband emission for coating on commercial GaN-based blue chips, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with green light emission was employed as the core to construct core-shell CQDs@ZIF-8 nanostructures. In addition, the molecular size of RhB is about 16.8 × 10.2 Å, matching the cavity size well, and it possesses outstanding red-light emission which can be an ideal guest unit for the core-shell structured nanophosphors.

CQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal reaction of the citric acid and urea in ultrapure water with the aid of microwave heating [45]. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) suggest the presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing groups (e.g., N–H, CO, C–H, –OH, C–O) for as-obtained CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the representative peaks at 3465, 2754, 1680, 1475 cm−1, corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption of the N–H, C–H, CO and C–O bands in CQDs, respectively, demonstrating the successful preparation of CQDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis reveals that the as-synthesized CQDs feature a homogeneous diameter distribution of about 12 nm (Fig. S2). We first synthesized the single-phase single-shell CQDs@ZIF-8, RhB@ZIF-8, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8 to confirm that ZIF-8 is capable of encapsulating or capturing different kinds of luminescent guests in situ. A certain amount of CQDs, RhB molecules and the mixture of CQDs and RhB were added to the precursor solution of ZIF-8, respectively, and reacted under the given conditions to harvest the corresponding nanomaterials. The produced nanocrystals were washed with methanol several times to remove the residual CQDs and RhB molecules on the surface of the crystals. No characteristic absorption peaks of CQD and RhB molecules were detected in the replaced methanol solutions. In addition, the single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 can be obtained through double-solvent mediated overgrowth approach by employing CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8 as the precursors [8,44]. As has been proved that it is possible to confine different guest units into different shells on a multi-shell ZIF-8 nanocrystal, in which the Förster energy transfer between two luminescent guests can be significantly suppressed by the solid shell with sufficiently large spatial separation [42]. Thus, we developed a single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 where the CQDs and the RhB are encapsulated successively into ZIF-8 through shell-by-shell epitaxy growth and segregated by solid ZIF-8 shells. The as-prepared nanocrystal CQDs@ZIF-82, RhB@ZIF-82, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82, and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 were characterized by powder XRD. From the XRD patterns in Fig. 2 a, it can be observed that all as-prepared materials exhibit the typical peaks of ZIF-8, indicating the preservation of the ZIF-8 crystal structure after encapsulating the guests of CQDs and RhB. It is necessary to note that characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs cannot be observed in relevant samples, which could be attributed to low content. We employed FTIR technique to investigate the chemical groups of the as-prepared materials. As shown in Fig. S3, in addition to the typical peak δsC-H of ZIF-8, the characteristic peaks, νN-H and νasC-H, for CQDs can be seen in CQDs@ZIF-82 and the peak νO-H can be observed in RhB@ZIF-82, which suggest that the guests were successfully encapsulated into the ZIF-8 host. Fig. 2b depicts the representative FTIR spectra of and CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 along with the spectra of ZIF-8, CQDs, and RhB for comparison. As can been seen that both the CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 display the typical peaks of the three precursors, demonstrating the successful preparation of single-phase multi-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocrystals. Furthermore, we employed scanning electron microscope (SEM) to confirm the formation of multi-shell structure. Fig. 3 depicts the SEM images of various intermediates for CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82, obtained based on sequential shell-by-shell overgrowth under double-solvent mediated environment. Specifically, the single-phase single-shell CQDs@ZIF-8 shows a truncated cube shape and a mean size of about 80 nm (Fig. 3a), then the nanocrystal was redispersed and grew in the given solvent condition to harvest single-phase single-shell CQDs@ZIF-82 nanocrystal, which features the same truncated cube shape but a larger averaged size of about 270 nm (Fig. 3b). With the implement of the third and fourth shell-by-shell overgrowth, uniform and truncated cube shaped CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-8 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocrystals were obtained, which show the averaged size of 390 and 550 nm (Fig. 3c and d), respectively. Furthermore, it is note that all the obtained nanocrystals in this consecutive synthesis process have a very similar morphology of uniform and truncate cube, as well as the smooth surface, demonstrating that the sequential growth and multi-shell structure do not affect the crystal quality.

The CQDs methanol solution was utilized to evaluated the luminescent properties of CQDs at room temperature. Fig. S4 presents the emission spectra of CQDs methanol solution excited at different excitation wavelengths. Apparently, upon excitation of 360 nm, CQDs exhibits a broad green emission band centered at 525 nm. The inset of Fig. S4 shows the bright fluorescent green of the CQDs methanol solution under irradiation of 360 nm-UV light. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission intensity of CQDs gradually increases and the maximum peak shifts toward the red end of the spectrum; whereas under the excitation of 460 nm, CQDs displays a strong and broad emission band centered around 550 nm. Such apparent excitation-dependent fluorescence can be attributed to the surface functional groups or defects induced by the abundant N and O elements of the precursors of citric acid and urea [45]. To explore the influence of solid ZIF-8 shell on guest luminous units, we prepared the CQDs@ZIF-82 single-phase single-shell nanocrystals and investigated its excitation-dependent fluorescence. It is necessary to note that a pure ZIF-8 shell was coated on the surface of the CQDs@ZIF-8 to form the single-phase single-shell CQDs@ZIF-82 through double-solvent method, which is done to avoid the influence of surface-absorbed guest units on the luminescence of composite materials. In addition, the thickness of the solid ZIF-8 shell has no obvious influence on the light quality. Fig. S5 suggests that CQDs@ZIF-82 has the strongest fluorescence emission with the initial input amount of CQDs methanol solution of 3.0 mL. This optimal CQDs amount was employed in the following process. As shown in Fig. 4 a, CQDs@ZIF-82 shows a broad blue emission band centered at 450 nm upon excitation at 360 nm, exhibiting a strong blue shift of 75 nm compared with that of CQDs methanol solution. Such distinct blue shift is probably due to the change of surface environment of CQDs and the confinement caused by the solid ZIF-8 shell. In addition, as the excitation wavelength increases, CQDs@ZIF-82 undergone a red shift to long wavelengths and shows a bright fluorescent green emission (the inset of Fig. 4a) centered around at 510 nm under excitation at 460 nm. The CIE chromaticity diagram coordinate of Fig. S6 shows the fluorescent color changing blue-green (excited at 360 nm) to yellow-green (excited at 460 nm); the corresponding CIE (x, y) color coordinates change from (0.23, 0.51) to (0.27, 0.55). Such results indicate that CQDs@ZIF-82 can serve as a green emitter to construct the high-quality white light emission excited by blue GaN-based chips.

Rhodamine B (RhB), is a representative high-efficiency organic fluorescent dye with bright red emission, arousing a range of interest and being explored in various fields [39]. It thus can be an ideal red emission unit for developing our host-guest luminescent composites. The excitation-dependent fluorescence of RhB aqueous solution was measured and showed in Fig. S7. Under excitation at 360 nm, RhB shows a red emission band centered at 586 nm, and the emission peak shifts toward long wavelength with the increasing excitation wavelength; whereas upon excitation at 460 nm, RhB exhibits a strong red emission (the insect of Fig. S7) with the peak of 610 nm. The one-pot self-assembly approach was utilized to capture and confine RhB molecules into the cavities of ZIF-8 host, and which was treated by double-solvent mediated overgrowth to harvest the RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposites. It is known that fluorescent dyes suffer from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect when the concentrations are too high or in the solid state, encapsulating suitable amount of RhB molecules into the cavities of ZIF-8 can effectively diminish the ACQ effect and solid-state luminescence can be achieved. As shown in Fig. S8, under excitation at 460 nm, the emission intensity of RhB@ZIF-82 increases at first and then decrease with the initial input RhB solution (5 mM) increases from 20 to 240 μL. Because the sample obtained with 120 μL RhB solution shows the strongest fluorescence, it was employed to evaluate the fluorescence property of RhB@ZIF-82 nanocrystals. Fig. 4b presents the excitation-dependent fluorescence of RhB@ZIF-82. Compared with the RhB aqueous solution, RhB@ZIF-82 shows a slight blue shift under the excitation wavelength varying from 360 to 460 nm, which can be attributed the cavity confinement of RhB within ZIF-8. Fig. S9 presents the CIE (0.57, 0.42) color coordinate of RhB@ZIF-82, which is close to the saturated red emitter (0.66, 0.33). Specifically, under 460 nm excitation, RhB@ZIF-82 displays a bright fluorescent red (the insect of Fig. 4b) with a maximum peak of 575 nm, suggesting RhB@ZIF-82 is a good red emitter candidate to form white light emission based on the commercial blue GaN-based chips.

The favorable fluorescence colors of CQDs@ZIF-82 and RhB @ZIF-82 inspired us to evaluate the possibility of simultaneously encapsulating the two emitters into ZIF-8 host (model 1 in Scheme 1) to achieve white light emission. By mixing CQDs and RhB in the precursor solution of ZIF-8, the CQDs&RhB@ZIF-8 was synthesized and then followed by double-solvent mediated overgrowth to obtain the single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 nanocrystal, for which the emission color can be tuned and optimized by changing the input concentration of RhB molecules under the given amount of CQDs methanol solution (3.0 mL). As expected, by elaborately modulating the concentration of RhB molecules, a series of CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposites (denoted as A1, A2 and A3) with bright yellow emission colors were prepared (Fig. 5 a). For instance, employing 3 mL of CQDs methanol solution and different amount (120 μL, blue line, A1; 200 μL, green line, A2; 240 μL, red line A3) of 5 mM RhB for the preparation, three nanocomposites result in emission spectra that cover the light range from cyan (480 nm) to red (650 nm) under the excitation at 460 nm. The corresponding CIE (x, y) chromaticity coordinates are determined as (0.36, 0.57), (0.38, 0.53) and (0.42, 0.51), respectively, which indicates that such mode can be applied as a strategy to achieve the yellow nanophosphor for white LED by adjusting the guest luminescence units. Importantly, the obtained nanocomposite A3 has the CIE color coordinate (0.42,0.51), which is very close to the CIE coordinate (0.42, 0.53) of the commercial yellow phosphor YAG:Ce3+. The quantum yield of nanocomposite A3 was determined as 5.78% (Table S1). It needs to be particularly pointed that the emission of RhB molecules in CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 were not only excited by the 460 nm blue light, but also can be further sensitized by the CQDs within the same ZIF-8 host. Fig. 4c presents the emission spectrum of CQDs along with the absorption spectrum of RhB for comparison. Obviously, there is a broad spectral overlap between the CQDs emission and RhB absorption, suggesting the efficient energy transfer from CQDs to RhB in CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 can occur via the FRET mechanism. Such kind of interaction, however, might decrease the luminescence efficiency of the nanocomposites. To overcome this negative factor, another strategy single-phase multi-shell (model 2 in Scheme 1) was applied to fabricate the nanocomposite.

A recent study has proved that separating different fluorescence dyes into porous framework with large spatial distance can effectively decrease the FRET interactions among the guest dye molecules [41]. Enlightened by this conclusion, we reasonably supposed that encapsulating CQDs and RhB molecules into the different layers of nanostructured multi-shell ZIF-8 with sufficiently large spatial distance, in which the FRET between the CQDs and RhB can be totally prevented by the solid shell and the photoluminescence intensity of the nanocomposite can be significantly improved. Therefore, we prepared the single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposite (Scheme 1) where the CQDs was encapsulated into the core-layer of double-shell ZIF-8 nanocrystals and the RhB molecules was subsequently accommodated into the out-layer of the multi-shell nanocrystals through sequential shell-by-shell overgrowth. Specifically, the prepared CQDs@ZIF-82 was used as core to overgrow the RhB@ZIF-8 with different concentrations of RhB, and the thus-harvested CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-8 was then employed as the new core to further overgrow to form the multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanophosphor for WLED. The emission color of the obtained materials under the irradiation of UV light varied from green to yellow with the formation of the CQDs@ZIF-82 and CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 which implied that RhB and carbon dots were successful encapsulated within the ZIF-8 pore. Moreover, the overgrowth process of the multi-shell was monitored by SEM (Fig. 3a, b, c, d), it can be observed that the size of the nanomaterials increased by step-by-step overgrowth in methanol and water and the morphology almost remained unchanged. The luminescent CQDs and RhB molecules was spatially separated with a large distance of about 300 nm by ZIF-8 shells, which is large enough to prevent the FRET interactions between the CQDs and RhB and facilitate the luminescence. The content of CQDs was maintained at 3 mL and the concentration of the RhB was then varied to explore its influence on the emission color of CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 nanocomposites. Three samples were obtained and denoted as B1, B2 and B3, which correspond to the input RhB amount of 120, 200 and 240 μL, respectively. As is shown in Fig. 5b, with the increase of the RhB, the fluorescence intensity of the RhB in CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 powder was increased. Moreover, the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5d) of sample B1, B2 and B3 were determined as (0.36, 0.50), (0.36, 0.49), and (0.39, 0.48), respectively, implying that the mode of single-phase multi-shell can be effectually applied for synthesizing nanocomposite materials with tunable emission color. Among them, the nanomaterials B3 shows the ideal CIE color coordinate, which is very close to that of the emission color of the commercial YAG: Ce phosphor that could be coated on the GaN-based chips for white-light emitting. Fig. S10 shows that the fluorescence spectra of the obtained nanocomposites A3 and B3 have wider emission band than that of YAG: Ce phosphor.

By comparing the fluorescence spectra of the two types of nanocomposites (Fig. 5a and b), one can see that the mode of single-phase multi-shell can effectively repress the interactions between the luminescent guests. For the single-phase single-shell mode, with the increasing concentration of RhB, the fluorescence intensity of RhB exhibited obvious enhancement while the luminescence of CQDs significantly decreased. Different from the single-phase single-shell materials, the emission intensity of CQDs for single-phase multi-shell nanocomposites, B1, B2, and B3, almost remained unchanged. This phenomenon indicated that FRET did not occur in the single-phase multi-shell nanocomposites which may be due to the multi-shell ZIF-8 nanocomposites provided efficiently large spatial separation to effectively avoid the occurrence of FRET. In order to confirm this point, the luminescence decay of CQDs@ZIF-82, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 (A3), and CQDs@ZIF-82@ CQDs@ZIF-82 (B3) were measured and shown in Fig. S10. Clearly, one can see that the lifetime of CQD (490 nm) of single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@ CQDs@ZIF-82 is determined as 8.42 ns, which is very close to that of CQDs@ZIF-82 (8.53 ns). More importantly, CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82 has a much shorter CQDs lifetime (6.86 nm) than that of CQDs@ZIF-82, confirming the efficient FRET interaction between CQDs and RhB in the single-phase single-shell systems. The quantum yield of the as-prepared materials was shown in Table S1. It reveals that the quantum yield of single-phase multi-shell samples B1, B2 and B3 are higher than that of the single-phase single-shell samples A1, A2 and A3. Specifically, compared with the single-phase single-shell nanocomposite A3 (5.78%), the quantum yield of the single-phase multi-shell B3 was up to 9.02%, implying that the multilayers mode can significantly avoid the energy transfer between different guest units and improve the luminescence performance of the composite materials.

In order to investigate the chemical stability of the synthesized nanocomposites, the prepared materials were checked in air, water, and NaOH (pH = 9, 12) at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S12, both two modes of the nanocomposites exhibit unchanged structures, suggesting their excellent chemical stability. In addition, after being exposed in air for six months and treated at 90 °C for several hours, the fluorescence emission spectra single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 were recorded and shown in Fig. S13, presents its long-term photostability. To further illustrate the potential of single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 for practical WLEDs, the nanocomposite materials were directly coated on commercially available blue LED chips (460 nm) to fabricate WLED lamps. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , upon applying a working voltage of 3.0 V and a current of 0.5 mA, the fabricated WLED lamps exhibits bright white light. Thus, single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82, as a kind of new single-phase white emission nanocomposite, is very fascinating for solid lighting applications.

In summary, we demonstrated a step-by-step method to developing CQDs&Dye@MOF-based host-guest nanocomposites for fabricating blue-light excited WLEDs through the in situ encapsulation of CQDs and RhB molecules into ZIF-8 cavities during its crystal formation. We investigated two strategies in which the CQDs and RhB dye molecules are encapsulated in different solid shells of the ZIF-8 host. In order to minimize the efficiency decrease caused by the interactions between CQDs and RhB molecules, the strategy of single-phase multi-shell was used to constructing the CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 which can isolate the CQDs and the RhB molecules in different solid ZIF-8 shells with a large spatial distance and validly suppress FRET process between CQDs and dyes. By adjusting the concentration of each luminescence unit, the nanocomposites synthesized based on the two modes all can obtain high quality stable white light emission, and the single-phase multi-shell CQDs@ZIF-82@RhB@ZIF-82 shows a higher quantum yield than that of the single-phase single-shell CQDs&RhB@ZIF-82. In view of the mild and unsophisticated synthesis process, the use of cheap and readily available raw materials, and systematic luminescence color tunability, such strategy has provided a promising approach to achieving highly solution-processable guest@MOF-based nanocomposites that can be useful in energy-efficient lighting, display and backlight devices.

Yantong Lu: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Shuo Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Kuangli Yu: Investigation. Jiulong Yu: Investigation. Dian Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chunxia Li: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
